# Preface {.unnumbered}

This book is for public health practitioners who already know how to do
the work — the epidemiology, the surveillance, the briefings — but are
being asked to do it in R. Maybe your agency is moving off SAS. Maybe a
collaborator handed you a `.R` file and you want to understand it. Maybe
you've opened RStudio, stared at a blank script, and closed it again.

This book is not a general introduction to R. It is not a statistics
textbook. It will not teach you epidemiology. It assumes you already
have domain knowledge and gives you the R fluency to apply it.

This book has one job: get a practicing epidemiologist writing, 
running, and sharing R-based analyses as fast as possible.

## How this book works

Every chapter builds on one analysis: a COVID-19 briefing for a state
health commissioner. You start with a raw CSV and a blank folder. By the
end, you have a self-updating report that pulls live data and publishes
itself. Each chapter introduces exactly one new skill and shows you why
it earns its place in the workflow — not as an exercise, but as a real
improvement to something you built yourself in the chapter before.

Work through the chapters in order. They are designed to be read with
R open in front of you, running the code as you go.


## What you need to bring

- R and Quarto installed on your machine
- RStudio or Positron as your editor
- A folder where you keep projects
- Epidemiological concepts you already know — rates, counts, surveillance cadence

You do not need prior programming experience. You do not need to clone
this repository. Everything you need to run is in the book itself.
